| **RS cutout — southern Brazil** | 255 in box (**only 8 with TMIN**) | **7.5k** TMIN obs | very sparse | only the **climatology** of the flip (§6.4); cannot model |

> The jump **3 billion → 40 million → 7 thousand** rows is the project in one sentence: cutting is not
> throwing data away — it's **spending the compute budget where there is signal** (Corn Belt, dense)
> and recognizing where the data simply **does not exist** (RS, sparse).

### 9.4 · Methods to **avoid reprocessing** the data (the point of the medallion)

Reprocessing 111 M rows on every tweak would be prohibitive. The **medallion** architecture exists to
avoid that — each layer is **persisted in Parquet** and only **what changed** is recomputed:

- **Isolated layers.** Changed a cleaning rule? Re-run **only silver + gold** — bronze (111 M rows,
  downloaded from the internet) is **never** redone. That's exactly what we did when adding the
  neighbourhood (regional) features: we rebuilt gold in ~3 min without touching bronze/silver.
- **Idempotent / append ingestion.** `stream_ingest` only downloads the **missing years**.
- **Partitioning by year** (`partitionBy("year")`): Spark reads only the needed partitions (*partition
  pruning*) and Parquet only the requested columns (*column pruning*) — it doesn't scan everything.
- **Small pre-computed, versioned artifacts:** southern flip (`southern_frost.csv`), global frost grid
  and `perf_stats.json` are ready in the repo; the charts **read** them instead of recomputing — which
  is why this report re-renders in **seconds**.
- **Guarded ingestion cell:** in the notebook, ingestion only runs if `data/bronze` does not exist.

> The expensive part (ingest and cross-join) runs **once** and is saved; the cheap part (charts, new
> features) runs on top of what's already ready.

### 9.5 · Where the cost is — and the power (and the limit) of distributed computing

**The most intensive step is building GOLD.** Lags, rolling means and GDD are **window functions**
(`Window.partitionBy("id").orderBy("date")`): for each station, Spark **re-sorts the whole series in
time** — a *wide transformation* that triggers **shuffle + sort** over 40 M rows, the part that
consumes the most memory per task. (Ingestion is heavy on **I/O**, not CPU; the `CrossValidator` is
heavy because it trains **12 models**, but over a sample.)

**The power:** Spark processes **111 M rows on a single laptop**, parallelizing by partitions —
unthinkable in Pandas/Excel.

**The limit we lived in this session (honest):** the machine has **~15 GiB of RAM** and the driver
uses **11 GiB of heap**. When raising concurrency to `local[10]`/`local[*]`, the tasks **compete for
the same heap** and the gold window sort **blew up** (`SparkOutOfMemoryError`). We backed off to
**`local[8]`** (+ `shuffle.partitions = 1024`, smaller partitions) for gold to fit. It's the central
trade-off of running distributed on a single machine: **more parallelism ↔ less memory per task**.
(The *notebook* itself — lighter, over the already-built gold — runs fine even at `local[*]`; it's
only the **gold build** that requires `local[8]`.)

**How we'd actually scale:** a **cluster** (more executors/RAM) removes the memory ceiling;
*checkpointing* the windows would break the long lineage; **AQE** (already on) coalesces partitions on
its own; and filtering elements/columns early + **broadcast joins** (already done) reduce the shuffle
at the source.

### 9.6 · What if we want to add more data?

Thanks to the medallion (§9.4), extending is **cheap and localized** — we only recompute the affected
layer:

- **More years:** re-run `stream_ingest --start-year … --end-year …` (idempotent: downloads only what's
  missing) and then `build_silver` / `build_gold`, which **re-derive** over the existing bronze.
- **Another region:** change the `region:` block in `config.yaml` (or `stream_ingest --region` with
  another box) and repeat silver/gold — the **same code** runs on any cutout, unchanged.
- **Another source/variable:** download the small data separately, join it at the right layer via a
  *broadcast join* and re-derive **only gold** — without touching bronze/silver.
- **Whole corpus:** train with `--sample-fraction 1.0` on a **cluster** (more RAM/executors), without
  changing a single line of the pipeline — only the infrastructure.

> In all cases, the expensive part (bronze, 111 M rows) is **reused**; we recompute only what actually
> changed.

## 10 · Limitations, conclusions & next steps

**What the result says.** The tuned GBT beats the rule baseline and reasons exactly like a domain
expert: today's `TMIN`, the **neighbourhood mean TMIN** (`region_tmin_mean`, which became the 2nd most
important feature) and the coldest night of the last week (`tmin_c_roll7_min`) dominate the signal.
Adding the regional feature was the highest-return improvement — exactly what the previous baseline's
weak spatial importances indicated.

**Limitations (honest).**
- *The neighbourhood feature is a grid approximation* (~1°), not a true distance nearest-neighbour —
  good enough, but refinable (see next steps).
- *`F.lag(1)` is "previous row", not "previous calendar day"* — across multi-day gaps, a lag can reach
  >1 day back. Tree models tolerate it; strict time-series models don't.
- *Single global model* — one boundary for all stations; per-climate models would help.
- *Trained on a subsample* — the final metrics are on the complete test set, but a cluster run would
  train on the whole corpus.

**Covering the gaps where there are no stations — ECMWF data.** The biggest limitation we saw is
*geographic*: where the station network is sparse (southern Brazil, §6.4), there is simply no TMIN to
train or predict on. The solution used in practice is to swap point stations for **gridded data**,
which cover the globe **uniformly** — whether or not a station exists at the spot:

- **ERA5 (ECMWF reanalysis)** — ~80 years of hourly history on a grid (~31 km). Replaces station
  history to **train and evaluate** the model in regions like RS, where GHCN fails: each grid cell
  becomes a "virtual station" with complete TMIN/TMAX.
- **ECMWF OP (IFS operational forecast)** + ensemble — **forecasted** TMIN/TMAX on a ~9 km grid for the
  coming days. It's what lets you **operationalize** the frost alert at any agricultural point, even
  without a sensor on site.

Natural flow: **train with ERA5** (dense, global history), **serve with ECMWF OP** (tomorrow's
forecast) and use the GHCN stations as *ground truth* to **calibrate/validate**. That way the same
classifier reaches the regions left out today for lack of stations — exactly the gap the southern
Brazil section exposed. (Cost: ERA5/OP are voluminous — GRIB/NetCDF in TB — and would fit the same
medallion, only the ingestion now reads a grid instead of station CSVs.)

**Conclusion — what we actually learned.**

*Model results (no exaggeration).* The tuned GBT reaches **ROC-AUC ≈ 0.98**, but the honest reading
(§8.4–8.6) is that **next-day frost is almost pure thermal persistence**: a trivial rule (`TMIN ≤ 2
°C`) already goes far. The ML gain is not "much more accuracy", but rather **trading a bit of recall
for much more precision** and delivering an **adjustable probability** for the business cost (FN ≫ FP).
And the biggest jump came from **feature engineering** — the regional neighbourhood (`region_tmin_mean`,
2nd most important) — not from a more complex model; precipitation helped only **marginally** (§7.5).
The lesson: **understanding the problem pays more than stacking models.**

*PySpark's practicality at this volume.* Taking **111 M rows** (20 years × 5 elements) from ingestion
to model **on a single laptop** would be infeasible in Pandas/Excel; with Spark, each layer ran in
**minutes**. But the exercise also exposed the **real limit** of running distributed on a single
machine: the gold build **OOM'd** on `local[*]` and required backing off to `local[8]` (§9.5) —
parallelism and memory-per-task pull in opposite directions. Spark solves **scale, not magic**: memory
still rules.

*Lessons from the functions used.* The project exercised the big-data "vocabulary" end to end: long→wide
**pivot** and **broadcast join** to unify sources without *shuffle* (§3.1); **window functions** (`lag`,
rolling means, YTD accumulation and `lead` for the label **without leakage**) to give the data temporal
memory (§6); Spark ML's **`Pipeline`** (`Imputer → VectorAssembler → GBT`) with
`CrossValidator`/`ParamGridBuilder` for tuning (§7); and the discipline of **temporal split + baseline +
stratified evaluation** to avoid fooling ourselves with the metric (§8). The medallion tied it all
together — each layer persisted, recomputing only what changes (§9.4).

*From model to business — the question that matters.* In the end, everything serves a decision for
whoever operates the crop: **how much of the region's corn supply is at risk in this window — and before
everyone else knows?** The answer has the two halves that §8.7 and §8.8 assemble. **How much:** in the
pre-harvest window (Sep–Oct), **~13% of Corn Belt station-days already record frost**, and the risk is
**not uniform** — it concentrates on the northwest/high fringe (Dakotas, western Nebraska, northern
Minnesota/Wisconsin), with more than half the cells above the regional mean. **The "before everyone":**
it's the **D+1…D+15** horizon that turns that percentage into *days of lead time* — the window to
trigger harvest, drying and logistics and protect supply while the market hasn't yet priced the frost.
(With §8.8's honest caveat: we use station density as a *proxy* for exposure, not real corn tonnage —
the map says **where**, the model says **when**.)

In one line: a complete medallion pipeline over real, cloud-scale data, ending in an **honest**
classifier — and, more than the final number, **clarity about where the signal comes from (and where it
stops).**

**Next steps.** True nearest-neighbour (haversine/BallTree) · per-crop GDD thresholds · regression
target (`next_day_tmin_c` → calibrated P(frost)) · **cyclic seasonality via Fourier harmonics** (encode
day-of-year as sines/cosines, instead of raw calendar features) · **more regional data from other
sources** (state networks, ERA5/ECMWF grids) to densify coverage · **real corn tonnage per cell** (USDA
NASS) to weight the storage map (§8.8) by actual production, not station density · nightly refresh with
Airflow · scale to 30+ years (linear, no code change).

```{python}
spark.stop()
print("Spark session stopped. Notebook complete.")
```
